description: The provided image is not a valid scientific figure from the research on Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel cold rolling. It is a UI element consisting of a partial blue-yellow annular icon, a red bookmark-like shape inside the ring, and the text 'Check for updates' below the icon. No information related to the microstructure, texture evolution, mechanical property changes, or cold rolling processing of the investigated steel can be extracted or verified from this image, all relevant research details are only recorded in the accompanying text extraction content rather than the displayed visual content.

This figure contains two subplots of room-temperature tensile stress-strain curves for Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel prepared with different cold rolling reductions from 0% to 90%. Subplot (a) displays engineering stress (in MPa) against engineering strain (in %), while subplot (b) displays true stress (in MPa) against true strain. Hollow circles on each curve mark the necking point, and dashed lines connect these necking points across different samples. As cold rolling reduction increases, the steel strength gradually improves from ~570 MPa yield strength (as-quenched 0% reduction state) to ~2.0 GPa at 90% reduction, while uniform ductility decreases from over 75% to very low values, showing a typical strength-ductility trade-off. The true stress at necking remains similar for samples with low to medium rolling reductions (<50%), and sharply increases when rolling reduction exceeds 50%. The curves demonstrate that cold rolling effectively strengthens the low-density steel via deformation-induced microstructural evolution.

This figure presents the initial microstructure of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel before cold rolling. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure map with a 200 μm scale bar, showing grain orientation distribution relative to the rolling direction. Subfigure (b) is an EBSD phase map with a 200 μm scale bar, where blue represents austenite, black lines indicate high-angle grain boundaries (misorientation ≥ 15°), and red lines mark twin boundaries, revealing equiaxed austenite grains with existing annealing twins in the as-solution treated state. Subfigure (c) is a TEM dark-field image with a 50 nm scale bar, attached with a selected-area electron diffraction pattern (foil normal to [001]γ), verifying uniformly distributed nano-sized κ'-carbides in the austenite matrix. These results provide the baseline initial microstructure for subsequent analysis of cold rolling induced microstructure, texture evolution and mechanical property changes of the alloy.

This group of optical micrographs demonstrates the microstructure evolution of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel under different cold rolling reductions, with all subgraphs sharing a 100 μm scale bar, and the axes marked as rolling direction (RD) and normal direction (ND). Subgraph (a) corresponds to 10% cold rolling reduction, (b) to 30%, (c) to 50%, (d) to 70%, and (e) to 90%. For reductions of 10% to 50% (subgraphs a-c), the microstructure shows typical Taylor-lattice-type low energy dislocation structure formed by planar dislocation glide, with gradually refined slip band spacing and inhomogeneous deformation across grains with favorable orientations. For reductions of 70% to 90% (subgraphs d-e), abundant shear bands are observed, grains are significantly elongated along RD, and shear bands increase with reduction to erode almost the entire matrix. The figure supports the conclusion that the microstructure evolution during cold rolling of the steel is divided into two distinct stages.

This set of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images displays the deformation microstructures of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel under different cold rolling reductions. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the sample with 10% cold rolling reduction, where Taylor lattice structure formed by multiple slip bands is observed, and dislocation pile-ups at slip band intersections are marked by green arrows. Subfigure (b) shows the sample with 30% cold rolling reduction, where the density of slip bands increases obviously with an average spacing of 231 nm, while the overall Taylor lattice structure is retained. The comparison indicates that at cold rolling reduction ≤30%, microstructure evolution is dominated by slip band refinement from planar dislocation glide, and weak interaction between non-coplanar slip bands cannot completely block dislocation glide. This figure evidences that early cold rolling deformation of the steel is characterized by Taylor lattice formation via planar slip.

This figure presents TEM characterizations of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel after 50% cold rolling reduction. Subfigure (a) is a low-magnification bright-field TEM image (scale bar: 500 nm), marking microbands with average width of 166 nm via green dashed lines. Subfigure (b) is a high-magnification bright-field TEM image (scale bar: 100 nm), showing intermittent parallel linear structures inside grains. Subfigure (c) is the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the linear structure in (b), with foil normal along [011], identifying diffraction spots from austenite matrix (M: (1-11)ₘ, (111)ₜ/ₘ) and deformation twins (T: (-111)ₜ). Subfigure (d) is the dark-field TEM image (scale bar: 100 nm) acquired from the (-111)ₜ twin reflection, visualizing nano-sized deformation twins with average thickness of ~5.1 nm. The figure confirms the coexistence of microbands and nano-deformation twins in the high stacking fault energy (~108 mJ/m²) steel at 50% cold rolling, serving as key evidence for its deformation mechanism and copper-type to brass-type texture transition.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs displays deformation microstructures of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel under different cold rolling reductions. Subfigure (a) is the dark-field image of nano-twins at 70% cold rolling reduction (scale bar 200 nm), with an inserted selected area electron diffraction pattern confirming the twin structure, where the average twin thickness is 19.2 nm. Subfigure (b) is the bright-field image of shear bands at 70% cold rolling reduction (scale bar 1 μm), the inserted polycrystalline diffraction ring verifies ultrafine grains are contained in shear bands, and twin regions are also labeled. Subfigure (c) is the bright-field image of dislocation tangles at 90% cold rolling reduction (scale bar 1 μm), with an average dislocation tangle size of 0.49 μm. This figure reveals the evolution of deformation substructures from nano-twins, shear bands to dislocation tangles as cold rolling reduction increases, supporting the cold deformation mechanism of the studied steel.

This figure is a schematic illustration of main texture components observed in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling, consisting of three orientation distribution function sections at φ2=0°, φ2=45°, and φ2=65°. The φ2=0° section displays α-fiber texture with labeled components C, G, B, and RtG. The φ2=45° section shows τ-fiber and γ-fiber textures, with corresponding components E, F, Cu, CuT, RtG, B, G marked. The φ2=65° section presents the S texture component. This schematic defines all texture components involved in the investigation, supporting the conclusion that the steel texture evolves from copper-type to brass-type with increasing cold rolling reduction, and α-fiber texture becomes dominant at high rolling reduction.

This figure presents orientation distribution function (ODF) sections at φ2 = 0°, 45°, and 65° for Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel under different cold rolling reductions (10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%) to characterize texture evolution during cold deformation. Identified texture components include α-fiber located at the φ2=0° section, γ-fiber located at the φ2=45° section, and specific components B, G, Cu, CuT, S, F. The overall texture intensity increases with rising cold rolling reduction, and is weaker than that of copper alloys and nickel as reported. A transition from copper-type texture to brass-type texture occurs at 50% cold rolling reduction, featured by the gradual decrease of Cu and S texture components. α-fiber becomes the dominant texture when cold rolling reduction reaches 90%, accompanied by weak γ-fiber. This figure supports the conclusion that cold rolling induces texture transition in the tested low-density steel, and its weak texture is related to planar dislocation glide and inhomogeneous deformation substructures.

This set of 6 plots displays texture evolution of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel under 10%, 30%, 50%, 70% and 90% cold rolling reductions. Subplots (a)-(d) present orientation density f(g) of α-fiber (<110>//ND), τ-fiber (<110>//TD), γ-fiber (<111>//ND) and β-fiber respectively, against corresponding Euler angles. α-fiber Brass orientation intensity rises with increasing reduction, with its peak position shifting from φ₁=45° to 20° at reduction >50%. τ-fiber Cu orientation peaks at 50% reduction then decreases, while Goss orientation increases continuously, with weak CuT component detected at 70% reduction. γ-fiber remains weak overall, only slightly enhanced at 90% reduction. β-fiber Cu/S orientations first increase then decrease, while Goss orientation rises monotonically. Subplots (e)-(f) show Φ and φ₁ positions of β-fiber maximum intensity, confirming a continuous shift from Brass to Goss with higher reduction. This figure supports the conclusion that texture transitions from Copper-type to Brass-type during cold rolling, contributed by planar slip and deformation twinning.
